schools.summary: Matthew Henry counsels against perplexity over the difficulties: Ezra copied the tribal lists as he found them, mending nothing, that the returned captives might resettle by family — and the names of the Israel of God are kept in everlasting remembrance, a figure of the Lamb’s book of life.

Keil and Delitzsch find the enumeration remarkable against Genesis 46:21, taking Aharah for the Ahiram of Numbers 26:38 and reading only the founders of families, Nohah and Rapha answering to Shephupham and Hupham.
Gill: the tribe of Benjamin is reviewed because it clung to Judah in worship and returned from exile with it, but chiefly for the sake of Saul, in whose line the register ends.
Clarke lays out the chapter’s plan — Benjamin to Saul through v.32, then Saul’s descendants — referring back to ch. 7:6.

Keil and Delitzsch make the double occurrence of Gera the key to the chapter: “sons” means founders of fathers’-houses, so cousins may share a name and Addar, Shephuphan, Huram differ from Ard, Shephupham, Hupham of Numbers 26.
Gill reports the older equations — Nohah with Becher or Naaman, Rapha with Rosh — one clan known by more than one name.
Gill: Addar is the Ard of the older record and Gera too a grandson, though set down where they might be mistaken for sons — the register slides from father to grandson without marking the step.

Keil and Delitzsch make the elasticity a principle: “sons” reaches to founders of households, so Addar, Shephuphan, Huram may be cousins distinct from Ard, Shephupham, Hupham; Abihud occurs only here and Ahoah links to the Ahiah of v.7.

Keil and Delitzsch place the sons of Ehud as heads of fathers’-houses of Geba of Benjamin (the Levite town, 1 Chron. 6:45), still surviving as Jeba, whence Naaman, Ahiah, and Gera were carried captive to Manahath, Gera the author of the removal.
Gill matches the names to the law — Naaman the head of the Naamanites, Ahoah perhaps Ehi, Shephuphan and Huram the Shupham and Hupham — noting some read the last three as sons of Ehud.

Ellicott keeps the identity of Ehud open (perhaps the judge, perhaps the Abihud of v.3), reads a parenthesis to “he removed them,” and corrects “removed” to “carried captive,” the deportation word of 1 Chron. 5:26.
Gill denies this Ehud is the judge, taking him for a son of Huram, and reads the following names as a parenthesis.
Henry identifies him with Ehud the second judge, son of Gera (Judg. 3:15), whose conquest of Moab explains the tribe’s later footing there.
Wesley: the men are described by their dwellings, not their parents, because Benjamin’s genealogies were broken by the near-extirpation of Judges 20.

debates.question: Is the Ehud of this verse the judge Ehud son of Gera?

sides.position: Yes — Ehud the second judge, whose Moabite conquest seated his line in Moab
Not the judge — another Ehud, perhaps a son of Huram
Open — possibly identical, or the same as Abihud of v.3

schools.summary: Ellicott untangles the clause — Uzza and Ahihud, chiefs of Geba, were carried captive to Manahath by three clans led by Gera (perhaps ‘for the sake of peace,’ amid feuds); Keil carries the thread on to Shaharaim, father of many houses, driven at length to Moab.
Gill reads the three names as resuming the interrupted list of Ehud’s sons.

themes: Uzza and Ahihud carried to Manahath

schools.summary: Ellicott takes Shaharaim as expelled from Geba to Moab, supposes his name dropped from v.7, corrects the corrupt Hebrew (with the LXX), and reads the clan-language of marriage and divorce figuratively; Keil notes he lived long in Moab and fathered many houses.
Gill keeps Shaharaim within the house of Ehud, a brother of Ahihud.

Ellicott takes Hodesh for a new Moabite wife or settlement and hears Moab in the sons’ names — Mesha (cf. 2 Kings 3:4) and Malcham (cf. Jer. 49:1); Keil simply lists the seven sons as heads of fathers’-houses.
Gill rejects the Targum’s equation of Hodesh with Baara as wrong.
Clarke follows the Targum: Hodesh is Baara renamed, ‘newly espoused’ after Shaharaim had put her away and remarried her.

debates.question: Is Hodesh another name for the wife Baara?

sides.position: Yes — Baara renamed on being re-espoused (with the Targum)
No — a distinct wife (the new Moabite wife)

themes: Moabite names in a Benjamite house

schools.summary: Gill dates Abitub and Elpaal to the marriage with Hushim before Shaharaim divorced her — the elder line, distinct from the Moabite clans of Hodesh.
Ellicott glosses ‘heads of the fathers’ as chiefs of kindred clans, referring back to his note at v.6.

Ellicott identifies the builder as Shemer (cf. 1 Chron. 7:34), reads a parenthesis, and traces Ono (Kefr Auna, cf. Ezra 2:33; Neh. 11:35) and Lod (the Lydda of Acts 9:32, now Ludd) into later history.
Clarke cites the Targum that Ono and Lod were ravaged and burnt in the war on Benjamin at Gibeah — the building read as a rebuilding after that ruin.
Gill adds Eber, Misham, and Shamed to the sons of Elpaal.

themes: the building of Ono and Lod

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the emphatic pronouns — the clans of Beriah and Shema, heads of Aijalon (Yalo), put to flight a Gittite population and took their place — and distinguishes this Beriah from the Ephraimite (7:23) and Asherite (7:30) namesakes.
Henry reads the rout of Gath as a reprisal for the Gittite slaying of Ephraim’s sons (1 Chron. 7:21), the name Beriah itself keeping the memory of that injury.
Gill takes Beriah and Shema as sons of Elpaal.

themes: driving out the men of Gath

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch reject Bertheau’s attempt to identify Elpaal’s sons Meshullam, Heber, Ishmerai with the Misham, Eber, Shemer of v.12 — it is doubtful the two Elpaals are even one man; of these descendants nothing further is known, and v.28 gathers them all as heads of houses.
Gill enrolls Ahio, Shashak, and Jerimoth among the sons of Elpaal.
Gill ties the thread: all that follow in this verse and the next are sons of Beriah the son of Elpaal.
Keil and Delitzsch count six sons of Beriah and note the names are found as his sons nowhere else.
Henry’s opening reflection carries over — such obscure men are the Jasher and Jeshurun, the upright ones, and the memory of the just is blessed.
Keil and Delitzsch place Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hezeki, and Heber among Elpaal’s sons and hold Meshullam and Heber apart from the similar Misham and Eber, refusing Bertheau’s fusion.
Gill links this verse and the next into one household — ‘these, with those that follow.’
Keil and Delitzsch number nine sons of Shimhi across these verses and confess nothing further is known of them, though some names occur elsewhere singly.
Gill marks the fresh cluster — ‘Jakim, with all the rest in these verses.’

themes: God keeps account of the obscure

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch number eleven sons of Shashak and confess nothing further is known of them.
Gill sweeps up the whole cluster — ‘Ishpan, and Heber, and all that follow to the end of these verses.’
Henry’s lesson frames the teeming list: God’s honour to raise up the diminished — the tribe once cut to six hundred men now swarming with clans.
Keil and Delitzsch count six sons of Jeroham, the last household before the summation, of whom nothing further is known — all to be gathered under v.28.
Gill keeps the line unbroken with his briefest connective, ‘who, with those next mentioned.’

Ellicott reads the doubled ‘heads’ as a warning that these are chiefs of clans, not individuals (cf. the ‘heads’ of Judg. 7:16), gathering vv.14–27 and contrasting the Jerusalem branches with those at Geba, Moab, Lod, Ono, and Aijalon.

Wesley: the dwelling in Jerusalem is noted to draw the returning exiles to resettle the perilous city, and the preserved names figure the Lamb’s book of life; Clarke notes Kennicott’s labor over the corrupt text that follows.
Gill takes the heads of the fathers as all the sons of Elpaal and their sons from v.14 on.

themes: names in the Lamb’s book of life

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch fix Saul’s ancestors at Gibeon (El Jib), name the father Jehiel (9:35) and his wife Maachah, and note two names — Ner and Mikloth — have dropped from the eight sons here against the ten of 9:36; Ellicott adds Jehiel’s omission and the collective ‘father of Gibeon.’
Gill names the father of Gibeon its builder and prince, Jehiel.
Clarke uses the divergence from 9:35-44 to fault Ezra’s text-critical method — two copies inserted, faults left standing.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note Ner has fallen out between Baal and Nadab (cf. 9:36); Ellicott dwells on ‘Baal,’ once innocently applied to Jehovah (Hosea 2:16) before idolatry defiled it and Adon replaced it, foreshadowing Eshbaal and Merib-baal.
Gill names Abdon the firstborn of Jehiel, prince of Gibeon.

themes: the name Baal and its history

schools.summary: Ellicott equates Shimeah with the older form Shimeam (9:38), reads that only the Mikloth-Shimeah branch of Gibeon settled in Jerusalem (perhaps at the Restoration, Neh. 11:1), and renders ‘before their brethren’ as ‘to the east,’ dwelling with the other Benjamite clans of v.28.
Gill notes Shimeah is the Shimeam of the parallel.

themes: the branch of Gibeon in Jerusalem
the king’s house among its kinsmen

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil resolve the Ner/Kish pedigree by omitted intermediate names (Ner perhaps a clan), gather Saul’s sons against 1 Sam. 14:49 and 31:2 (Abinadab = Ishui, double naming), and explain Eshbaal/Ish-bosheth and Merib-baal/Mephibosheth as the purging of the defiled ‘Baal.’

Henry marks that no post-revolt kings of Israel are recorded, only Saul’s house, and that Saul’s line is preserved solely through Jonathan for the sake of his covenant with David (1 Sam. 20:15), down to Ulam.
Gill, with the Targum, makes Ner and Abiel one man under two names.

debates.question: How are Ner, Kish, and Abiel related?

sides.position: Ner and Abiel are the same man under two names
Kish is son of Ner and grandson of Abiel, ‘son’ used loosely
Intermediate names are omitted between Ner and Kish; Ner may be a clan

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch identify Meribbaal with the Mephibosheth of 2 Sam. 4:4; 9:6, gloss the changed form as ‘exterminans idolum,’ note his lameness, and trace the line through Micah down ten generations to the sons of Eshek.
Gill equates Meribbaal with Mephibosheth, as Jerubbaal became Jerubbesheth.
Clarke: the detested ‘Baal’ (lord) was changed to ‘bosheth’ (shame) in the honoured name.

Ellicott takes Tarea and Tahrea (9:41) for one name in harder and softer form; Keil sets Micah’s sons at the head of the ten generations to the chapter’s end, the subscription ‘all these of Benjamin’ pointing back to v.1.
Gill notes Tarea is the Tahrea of 9:41, where Ahaz is dropped but rightly supplied by the AV.

themes: the line bound back to v.1

schools.summary: Ellicott derives the parallel’s ‘Jarah’ from a d/r letter-confusion for Jeho’addah, and notes Alemeth appears elsewhere as a son of Becher (7:8) and a Levitical town (6:60) but is personal here.
Gill sets Jehoadah beside his variant Jarah in 9:42.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the descent from Moza through Binea by ‘begat’ and then by the formula ‘his son’ down to Azel, whose six sons follow.
Gill notes Rapha is the Rephaiah of the parallel at 9:43.

Ellicott suspects ‘Bocheru’ should be read ‘his firstborn’ (with some MSS, the LXX, and Syriac), the conjunction wanting and the form anomalous, and notes Azel was also a place near Jerusalem (Zech. 14:5; Micah 1:11).
Gill counts the six sons of Azel to their stated number.

Ellicott places Eshek as brother of Azel and son of the Eleasah of v.37, the historian developing the elder line through Ulam the firstborn; Keil and Delitzsch fold this collateral branch, with the direct line, back into Benjamin under the coming subscription.
Gill takes Eshek for Azel’s brother, otherwise unknown unless the conjectured Elasah.

Ellicott sets the archers in Benjamin’s old fame (Judg. 20:16; 3:15), reads ‘treaders of the bow’ (1 Chron. 5:18), and weighs their date — the 14th generation near Josiah, or more likely a house of the Return like Zerubbabel’s clans (Ezra 2:18), hearing the seed-royal Ishmael of 2 Kings 25:25 among Azel’s sons.
Henry prizes the hundred and fifty archers as qualified to serve their country — a better praise than pomp or wealth.
Gill: men of great fortitude and courage, their names unrecorded.
Clarke relays the Targum’s allegory of subduing lust as bending a bow, and mocks Jarchi’s ‘thirteen thousand camels’ of expositions.

outliers.note: Turns the verse into a jibe at rabbinic allegorizing — Jarchi’s expositions of Azel’s six sons ‘enough to load thirteen thousand camels’ — a shameful hyperbole he hopes the metaphor-mongers never recover.

themes: the tribe standing in its heirs

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch seal the sons of Beriah and again refuse Bertheau’s equation of Meshullam, Heber, Ishmerai with Misham, Eber, Shemer, doubting the two Elpaals are one.
Gill’s gloss on v.15 already gathers Michael, Ispah, and Joha as sons of Beriah.

Keil and Delitzsch close the sons of Elpaal and survey what follows — the sons of Shimhi, Shashak, and Jeroham, of whom nothing further is known — with v.28 gathering the whole as heads of the fathers.
Gill’s connective on v.17 already draws in these last sons of Elpaal.
Ellicott: Abitub and Elpaal are offshoots of Shaharaim by Hushim before her divorce, clans that settled near Lod/Lydda (v.12) and took no part in the emigration to Moab.
Gill marks them as born before Shaharaim sent Hushim away — the branch that stayed west of the Jordan.

themes: the fork in the family’s road

schools.summary: Ellicott notes Ahio supports the AV at v.14, Zacher is Zecher (the Zechariah of 9:37), and ‘and Mikloth’ has dropped out after Zecher through the repeated words that open v.32 (recovered from 9:33); Keil handles the same block of Jehiel’s sons.

Keil and Delitzsch place Elienai, Zilthai, and Eliel among the nine sons of Shimhi, of whom nothing further is known; Ellicott frames them by v.28 as heads of clans, not individuals — the doubled ‘heads’ guarding against a bare family tree.
Gill’s connective on v.19 enrolls them among ‘all the rest in these verses.’
